**Zoroastrianism**,[[a]](#cite_note-3) also called **Mazdayasna**[[b]](#cite_note-4) and **Behdin**,[[c]](#cite_note-5) is an [Iranian religion](/wiki/Iranian_religions "Iranian religions") centred on the [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") and the teachings of [Zarathushtra Spitama](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster"), who is more commonly referred to by the Greek translation, Zoroaster ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ζωροάστρις, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *Zōroastris*). Among the world's oldest organized faiths, its adherents exalt an [uncreated](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity"), [benevolent](/wiki/Omnibenevolence "Omnibenevolence"), and [all-wise deity](/wiki/List_of_knowledge_deities#Persian_mythology "List of knowledge deities") known as [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") ([Avestan](/wiki/Avestan_language "Avestan language"): 𐬀𐬵𐬎𐬭𐬀⸱ 𐬨𐬀𐬰𐬛𐬁), who is hailed as the supreme being of the universe. Opposed to Ahura Mazda is [Angra Mainyu](/wiki/Ahriman "Ahriman") (𐬀𐬢𐬭𐬀⸱ 𐬨𐬀𐬌𐬥𐬌𐬌𐬎), who is personified as a [destructive spirit](/wiki/List_of_death_deities#Persian-Zoroastrian "List of death deities") and the adversary of all things that are good. As such, the Zoroastrian religion combines a [dualistic cosmology](/wiki/Dualism_in_cosmology "Dualism in cosmology") of [good and evil](/wiki/Good_and_evil "Good and evil") with an [eschatological](/wiki/Eschatological "Eschatological") outlook predicting the [ultimate triumph of Ahura Mazda over evil](/wiki/Frashokereti "Frashokereti").[[1]](#cite_note-Boyd-1979-1) Opinions vary among scholars as to whether Zoroastrianism is [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheistic "Monotheistic"),[[1]](#cite_note-Boyd-1979-1) [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheistic "Polytheistic"),[[2]](#cite_note-Skjærvø_2005-2) [henotheistic](/wiki/Henotheistic "Henotheistic"),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkjærvø200515_with_footnote_1-6) or a combination of all three.[[4]](#cite_note-7) Zoroastrianism shaped [Iranian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Iran "Culture of Iran") and [history](/wiki/History_of_Iran "History of Iran"), while scholars differ on whether it significantly influenced ancient [Western philosophy](/wiki/Western_philosophy "Western philosophy") and the [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions"),[[5]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[6]](#cite_note-9) or gradually reconciled with other religions and traditions, such as [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[7]](#cite_note-10)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Originating from Zoroaster's reforms of the [ancient Iranian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Iranian_religion "Ancient Iranian religion"), Zoroastrianism began during the [Avestan period](/wiki/Avestan_period "Avestan period") (possibly as early as the 2nd millennium BCE), but was first recorded in the mid-6th century BCE. For the following millennium, it was the official religion of successive Iranian polities, beginning with the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), which formalized and institutionalized many of its tenets and rituals, and ending with the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), which revitalized the faith and standardized its teachings.[[8]](#cite_note-11) In the 7th century CE, the [rise of Islam](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") and the ensuing [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia") marked the beginning of the decline of Zoroastrianism. The [persecution of Zoroastrians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Zoroastrians "Persecution of Zoroastrians") by the [early Muslims](/wiki/Early_Muslims "Early Muslims") in the nascent [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") prompted much of the community to migrate to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), where they were granted asylum and became the progenitors of today's [Parsis](/wiki/Parsis "Parsis"). Once numbering in the millions, the world's total Zoroastrian population is estimated to comprise [between 110,000 and 120,000 people](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Zoroastrian_population "List of countries by Zoroastrian population"), with most of them residing either in [India](/wiki/Zoroastrianism_in_India "Zoroastrianism in India") (50,000–60,000), in [Iran](/wiki/Zoroastrianism_in_Iran "Zoroastrianism in Iran") (15,000–25,000), or in [North America](/wiki/FEZANA "FEZANA") (22,000). The religion is thought to be declining because of restrictions on conversion, strict [endogamy](/wiki/Endogamy "Endogamy"), casteism, and low birth rates. The low birth rates are present both among [Iranian Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrianism_in_Iran "Zoroastrianism in Iran") and the Parsis.

The central beliefs and practices of Zoroastrianism are contained in the Avesta, a compendium of sacred texts assembled over several centuries. Its oldest and most central component is the [Gathas](/wiki/Gatha_(Zoroaster) "Gatha (Zoroaster)"), purported to be the direct teachings of Zoroaster and his account of conversations with Ahura Mazda. These writings are part of a major section of the *Avesta* called the [Yasna](/wiki/Yasna "Yasna"), which forms the core of Zoroastrian liturgy. Zoroaster's religious philosophy divided the early Iranian gods of [Proto-Indo-Iranian paganism](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Iranian_paganism "Proto-Indo-Iranian paganism") into emanations of the natural world—the *[ahura](/wiki/Ahura "Ahura")* and the *[daeva](/wiki/Daeva "Daeva")*; the former class consisting of divinities to be revered and the latter class consisting of divinities to be rejected and condemned. Zoroaster proclaimed that Ahura Mazda was the supreme creator and sustaining force of the universe, working in *gētīg* (the visible material realm) and *mēnōg* (the invisible spiritual and mental realm) through the [Amesha Spenta](/wiki/Amesha_Spenta "Amesha Spenta"), a class of seven divine entities that represent various [aspects](/wiki/Aspect_(religion) "Aspect (religion)") of the universe and the highest moral good. Emanating from Ahura Mazda is Spenta Mainyu (the Holy or Bountiful Spirit), the source of life and goodness,[[9]](#cite_note-12) which is opposed by Angra Mainyu (the Destructive or Opposing Spirit), who is born from *[Aka Manah](/wiki/Aka_Manah "Aka Manah")* (evil thought). Angra Mainyu was further developed by [Middle Persian literature](/wiki/Middle_Persian_literature "Middle Persian literature") into Ahriman ([Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian_language "Middle Persian language"): 𐭠𐭧𐭫𐭬𐭭𐭩), Ahura Mazda's direct adversary. The two terms are synonymous.

Zoroastrian doctrine holds that, within this cosmic dichotomy, human beings have the choice between *[Asha](/wiki/Asha "Asha")* ('truth', 'cosmic order'), the principle of righteousness or "rightness" that is promoted and embodied by Ahura Mazda, and *Druj* ('falsehood', 'deceit'), the essential nature of Angra Mainyu that expresses itself as greed, wrath, and envy.[[10]](#cite_note-13) Thus, the central moral precepts of the religion are good thoughts (*humata*), good words (*hukhta*), and good deeds (*huvarshta*), which are recited in many prayers and ceremonies.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[11]](#cite_note-14)[[12]](#cite_note-15) Many of the practices and beliefs of ancient Iranian religion can still be seen in Zoroastrianism, such as reverence for nature and its elements, such as water (*[aban](/wiki/Aban "Aban")*). Fire (*[atar](/wiki/Atar "Atar")*) is held by Zoroastrians to be particularly sacred as a symbol of Ahura Mazda himself, serving as a focal point of many ceremonies and rituals, and serving as the basis for Zoroastrian places of worship, which are known as [fire temples](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple").

## Etymology

The name *Zoroaster* (Ζωροάστηρ) is a [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") rendering of the [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") name *Zarathustra*. He is known as *Zartosht* and *Zardosht* in [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and *Zaratosht* in [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language").[[13]](#cite_note-Schmitt-2002-16) The Zoroastrian name of the religion is *Mazdayasna*, which combines *Mazda-* with the Avestan word *[yasna](/wiki/Yasna "Yasna")*, meaning "worship, devotion".[[14]](#cite_note-Boyce_1983-17) In [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), an adherent of the faith is commonly called a Zoroastrian or a Zarathustrian. An older expression still used today is *Behdin*, meaning "of the good religion", deriving from *beh* < Middle Persian *weh* 'good' + *din* < Middle Persian *dēn* < Avestan *daēnā*".[[15]](#cite_note-18) In the Zoroastrian [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy"), this term is used as a title for a lay individual who has been formally inducted into the religion in a *[Navjote](/wiki/Navjote "Navjote")* ceremony, in contrast to the priestly titles of *osta*, *osti*, *[ervad](/wiki/Ervad "Ervad")* (hirbod), *[mobed](/wiki/Mobed "Mobed")* and *[dastur](/wiki/Dastur "Dastur")*.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

The first surviving reference to Zoroaster in English scholarship is attributed to [Thomas Browne](/wiki/Thomas_Browne "Thomas Browne") (1605–1682), who briefly refers to Zoroaster in his 1643 *[Religio Medici](/wiki/Religio_Medici "Religio Medici")*.[[17]](#cite_note-20) The term *Mazdaism* ([/ˈmæzdə.ɪzəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is an alternative form in English used as well for the faith, taking *Mazda-* from the name [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") and adding the suffix *-ism* to suggest a belief system.[[18]](#cite_note-21)

## Theology

See also: [Criticism of Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Criticism_of_Zoroastrianism "Criticism of Zoroastrianism") and [Religious influences on Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Religious_influences_on_Zoroastrianism "Religious influences on Zoroastrianism")

The theological category of Zoroastrianism is hard to define. The reasons are the difficulties in assigning precise dates to the principal texts and the fact that many contain much older material. Furthermore, Zoroastrianism shaped only slowly over time and was not complete even by the time of the [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia"). Polytheistic, monotheistic, and dualistic strands can be identified in the wider Zoroastrian tradition, with [dualism](/wiki/Dualism_in_cosmology "Dualism in cosmology") being the dominant tendency. The major difference to [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism") lies in the insistence of good in the creation account.[[19]](#cite_note-22)

Some scholars believe Zoroastrianism started as an Indo-Iranian polytheistic religion: according to Yujin Nagasawa, "like the rest of the Zoroastrian texts, the Old [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") does not teach monotheism".[[20]](#cite_note-23) By contrast, Md. Sayem characterizes Zoroastrianism as being one of the oldest monotheistic religions in the world.[[21]](#cite_note-auto-24)

Zoroastrians treat [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") as the supreme god, but believe in lesser divinities known as [yazatas](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata") ("beings worthy of worship"), who share some similarities with the [angels](/wiki/Angels "Angels") in Abrahamic religions.[[22]](#cite_note-25) These yazatas include [Anahita](/wiki/Anahita "Anahita"), [Sraosha](/wiki/Sraosha "Sraosha"), [Mithra](/wiki/Mithra "Mithra"), [Rashnu](/wiki/Rashnu "Rashnu"), and [Tishtrya](/wiki/Tishtrya "Tishtrya"). Historian [Richard Foltz](/wiki/Richard_Foltz "Richard Foltz") has put forth evidence that [Iranians of the pre-Islamic era](/wiki/History_of_Iran "History of Iran") worshipped all these figures; especially the gods [Mithra](/wiki/Mithra "Mithra") and [Anahita](/wiki/Anahita "Anahita").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoltz2013xiv-26)

[Prods Oktor Skjærvø](/wiki/Prods_Oktor_Skj%C3%A6rv%C3%B8 "Prods Oktor Skjærvø") states Zoroastrianism is henotheistic, and "a dualistic and polytheistic religion, but with one supreme god, who is the father of the ordered cosmos".[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkjærvø200515_with_footnote_1-6) Brian Arthur Brown states that this is unclear, because historic texts present a conflicting picture, ranging from Zoroastrianism's belief in "one god, two gods, or a best god henotheism".[[24]](#cite_note-27) Economist Mario Ferrero suggests that Zoroastrianism transitioned from polytheism to monotheism due to political and economic pressures.[[25]](#cite_note-Ferrero-2021-28)

In the 19th century, through contact with Western academics and missionaries, Zoroastrianism experienced a massive theological change that still affects it today. The [Rev. John Wilson](/wiki/John_Wilson_(Scottish_missionary) "John Wilson (Scottish missionary)") led various missionary campaigns in India against the Parsi community, disparaging the Parsis for their "[dualism](/wiki/Dualistic_cosmology "Dualistic cosmology")" and "polytheism" and as having unnecessary rituals while declaring the Avesta to not be "divinely inspired". This caused mass dismay in the relatively uneducated Parsi community, which blamed its priests and led to some conversions towards [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The arrival of the German orientalist and philologist [Martin Haug](/wiki/Martin_Haug "Martin Haug") led to a rallied defense of the faith through Haug's reinterpretation of the Avesta through Christianized and European orientalist lens. Haug postulated that Zoroastrianism was solely monotheistic with all other divinities reduced to the status of angels while Ahura Mazda became both omnipotent and the source of evil as well as good. Haug's thinking was subsequently disseminated as a Parsi interpretation, thus corroborating Haug's theory, and the idea became so popular that it is now almost universally accepted as doctrine (though being re-evaluated in modern Zoroastrianism and academia).[[26]](#cite_note-Hinnells-2007-29) It has been argued by Almut Hintze that this designation of monotheism is not wholly perfect and that Zoroastrianism instead has its "own form of monotheism" which combines elements of dualism and polytheism.[[27]](#cite_note-30) [Farhang Mehr](/wiki/Farhang_Mehr "Farhang Mehr") asserts that Zoroastrianism is principally monotheistic with some dualistic elements.[[28]](#cite_note-31)

Lenorant and Chevallier assert that Zoroastrianism's concept of divinity covers both being and mind as [immanent](/wiki/Immanent "Immanent") entities, describing Zoroastrianism as having a belief in an immanent self-creating universe with consciousness as its special attribute, thereby putting Zoroastrianism in the [pantheistic](/wiki/Pantheistic "Pantheistic") fold sharing its origin with [Indian Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_India "Hinduism in India").[[29]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-33)

## Nature of the divine

Zoroastrianism contains multiple classes of divine beings, who are typically organised into tiers and spheres of influence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ahuras

Main article: [Ahura](/wiki/Ahura "Ahura")

The class of divine beings known as [Ahuras](/wiki/Ahura "Ahura") were inherited from the concept of the *asura* in prehistoric Indo-Iranian religion. In the *Rig Veda*, the asuras are the "older gods" who ruled over the primordial chaos of the universe. This includes figures such as "Father Asura", [Varuna](/wiki/Varuna "Varuna"), and [Mitra](/wiki/Mithraism "Mithraism").[[31]](#cite_note-AHURA-34)

#### Ahura Mazda

Main article: [Ahuramazda](/wiki/Ahuramazda "Ahuramazda")

[Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda"), also known as Oromasdes, Ohrmazd, Ormazd, Ormusd, Hoormazd, Harzoo, Hormazd, Hormaz and Hurmz, is the [creator deity](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity") and the supreme god in Zoroastrianism. [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") stands for the dual deity *Mitrāˊ-Váruṇā* of the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") holy book known as the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda").[[31]](#cite_note-AHURA-34)

According to scholars, Ahura Mazda is an uncreated, omniscient, omnipotent and benevolent God who has created the spiritual and material existences out of infinite light, and maintains the cosmic law of Asha. He is the first and most invoked spirit in [Yasna](/wiki/Yasna "Yasna"), and is unrivaled, has no equals and presides over all creation.[[32]](#cite_note-Boyce-1983-35) In Avesta, Ahura Mazda is the only true God, and the representation of goodness, light, and truth. He is in conflict with the evil spirit Angra Mainyu, the representation of evil, darkness, and deceit. Angru Mainyu's goal is to tempt humans away from Ahura Mazda. Notably, Angra Mainyu is not a creation of Ahura Mazda but an independent entity.[[33]](#cite_note-36) The belief in Ahura Mazda, the "Lord of Wisdom" who is considered an all-encompassing Deity and the only existing one, is the foundation of Zoroastrianism.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

#### Ahura Mithra

Main article: [Mithraism](/wiki/Mithraism "Mithraism")

[Mitra](/wiki/Mitra "Mitra"), also called [Mithra](/wiki/Mithraism "Mithraism"), was originally an Indo-Iranian god of "covenant, agreement, treaty, alliance, promise." Mitra is considered a being worthy of worship and is "characterized by riches".[[35]](#cite_note-38)

### Yazata

Main article: [Yazatas](/wiki/Yazatas "Yazatas")

The [Yazata](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata") (Avestan: 𐬫𐬀𐬰𐬀𐬙𐬀) are divine beings worshiped by song and sacrifice in Zoroastrianism, in accordance with the [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta"). The word '[Yazata'](/wiki/Yazatas "Yazatas") is derived from 'Yazdan', the Old Persian word for 'god',[[36]](#cite_note-Büchner_1934-39) and literally means "divinity worthy of worship or veneration". As a concept, it also contains a wide range of other meanings; though generally signifying (or used as an epithet of) a divinity.[[37]](#cite_note-40)[[38]](#cite_note-41)

The origins of [Yazata](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata") are varied, with many also being featured as gods in [Hinduism](/wiki/List_of_Hindu_deities "List of Hindu deities"), or other [Iranian](/wiki/Iran "Iran") religions. In modern Zoroastrianism, the Yazata are considered holy emanations of the creator, always devoted to him and obey the will of [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda"). While subject to repression by the [Islamic Caliphate](/wiki/Islamic_Caliphate "Islamic Caliphate"), the [Yazata](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata") were often framed as "[angels](/wiki/Angel "Angel")" to counter accusation of [polytheism](/wiki/Shirk_(Islam) "Shirk (Islam)") (*shirk*).[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce2001[httpsbooksgooglecombooksida6gbxVfjtUECpgPA157_157]-42) According to the [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") The [Yazata](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata") assist [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") in his battle against the evil spirit, and are hypostases of moral or physical aspects of creation. The yazatas collectively are "the good powers under Ahura Mazda", who is "the greatest of the yazatas".[[36]](#cite_note-Büchner_1934-39)

#### Notable Yazatas

* [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda")
* [Mitra](/wiki/Mitra "Mitra") or [Mithra](/wiki/Mithra "Mithra")
* [Anahita](/wiki/Anahita "Anahita") – formerly an Iranian water goddess
* Ātar (Fire)
* [Rashnu](/wiki/Rashnu "Rashnu"), one of the three judges who pass judgment on the souls of people after death
* [Sraosha](/wiki/Sraosha "Sraosha") or Srōsh
* [Verethragna](/wiki/Verethragna "Verethragna") – who may be the Vedic god [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra")

#### Amesha Spentas

Yazatas are further divided into Amesha Spentas, their "ham-kar" or "Collaborators" who are Lower Ranking divinities,[[40]](#cite_note-auto1-43) and also certain healing plants, primordial creatures, the fravashis of the dead, and certain prayers that are themselves considered holy.

The Amesha Spentas and their "ham-kar" or "collaborator" Yazatas are as follows:

* [Vohu Manah](/wiki/Vohu_Manah "Vohu Manah") + Mah / Geush Urvan / Ram
* [Asha Vahishta](/wiki/Asha_Vahishta "Asha Vahishta") + Atar / Sraosha / Verethraghna
* [Kshatra Vairya](/wiki/Kshatra_Vairya "Kshatra Vairya") + Khwar / Mithra / Asman / Anaghran
* [Spenta Armaiti](/wiki/Spenta_Armaiti "Spenta Armaiti") + Ap / Daena / Ashi / Manthra Spenta
* [Haurvatat](/wiki/Haurvatat "Haurvatat") + Tishtriya / Fravashi / Vata
* [Ameretat](/wiki/Ameretat "Ameretat") + Rashnu / Arshtat / Zamyad[[40]](#cite_note-auto1-43)

## Principal beliefs

### Tenets of faith

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Faravahar.svg/250px-Faravahar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Faravahar.svg)

[Faravahar](/wiki/Faravahar "Faravahar"), one of the primary symbols of Zoroastrianism, believed to be the depiction of a *[Fravashi](/wiki/Fravashi "Fravashi")* or the *[Khvarenah](/wiki/Khvarenah "Khvarenah")*.

In Zoroastrianism, [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") is the beginning and the end, the creator of everything that can and cannot be seen, the eternal and uncreated, the all-good and source of Asha.[[14]](#cite_note-Boyce_1983-17) In the [Gathas](/wiki/Gathas "Gathas"), the most sacred texts of Zoroastrianism thought to have been composed by Zoroaster himself, Zoroaster acknowledged the highest devotion to Ahura Mazda, with worship and adoration also given to Ahura Mazda's manifestations ([Amesha Spenta](/wiki/Amesha_Spenta "Amesha Spenta")) and the other ahuras ([Yazata](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata")) that support Ahura Mazda.[[41]](#cite_note-44)

*[Daena](/wiki/Daena "Daena")* (*din* in modern [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and meaning "that which is seen") is representative of the sum of one's spiritual conscience and attributes, which through one's choice Asha is either strengthened or weakened in the Daena.[[42]](#cite_note-45) Traditionally, the *manthras* (similar to the Hindu sacred utterance *[mantra](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra")*) prayer formulas, are believed to be of immense power and the vehicles of Asha and creation used to maintain good and fight evil.[[43]](#cite_note-ZOROASTRIAN_RITUALS-46) *Daena* should not be confused with the fundamental principle of *[Asha](/wiki/Asha "Asha")*, believed to be the cosmic order which governs and permeates all existence, and the concept of which governed the life of the ancient Indo-Iranians. For these, *asha* was the course of everything observable—the motion of the planets and astral bodies; the progression of the seasons; and the pattern of daily nomadic herdsman life, governed by regular metronomic events such as sunrise and sunset, and was strengthened through truth-telling and following the Threefold Path.[[44]](#cite_note-AṦA-47)

All physical creation (*getig*) was thus determined to run according to a master plan—inherent to Ahura Mazda—and violations of the order (*druj*) were violations against creation, and thus violations against Ahura Mazda.[[45]](#cite_note-GĒTĪG_AND_MĒNŌG-48) This concept of *asha* versus the *druj* should not be confused with Western and especially Christian notions of good versus evil, for although both forms of opposition express moral conflict, the *asha* versus *druj* concept is more systemic and less personal, representing, for instance, chaos (that opposes order); or "uncreation", evident as natural decay (that opposes creation); or more simply "the lie" (that opposes truth and goodness).[[44]](#cite_note-AṦA-47) Moreover, in the role as the one uncreated creator of all, Ahura Mazda is not the creator of *druj*, which is "nothing", anti-creation, and thus (likewise) uncreated and developed as the antithesis of existence through choice.[[46]](#cite_note-Druj-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Parsee_Wedding_1905.jpg/250px-Parsee_Wedding_1905.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parsee_Wedding_1905.jpg)

A *[Parsi](/wiki/Parsi_people "Parsi people")* Wedding, 1905

In this [schema](/wiki/Model_(abstract) "Model (abstract)") of *asha* versus *druj*, mortal beings (both humans and animals) play a critical role, for they too are created. Here, in their lives, they are active participants in the conflict, and it is their spiritual duty to defend Asha, which is under constant assault and would decay in strength without counter*action*.[[44]](#cite_note-AṦA-47) Throughout the [Gathas](/wiki/Gathas "Gathas"), Zoroaster emphasizes deeds and actions within society and accordingly extreme [asceticism](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism") is frowned upon in Zoroastrianism but moderate forms are allowed within.[[47]](#cite_note-DARVĪŠ-50)

*Humata*, *Huxta*, *Huvarshta* (Good Thoughts, Good Words, Good Deeds), the **Threefold Path of Asha**, is considered the core maxim of Zoroastrianism especially by modern practitioners. In Zoroastrianism, good transpires for those who do righteous deeds for its own sake, not for the search of reward. Those who do evil are said to be attacked and confused by the druj and are responsible for aligning themselves back to Asha by following this path.[[48]](#cite_note-HUMATA_HŪXTA_HUVARŠTA-51) There is also a heavy emphasis on spreading happiness, mostly through charity,[[49]](#cite_note-CHARITABLE_FOUNDATIONS-52) and respecting the spiritual equality and duty of both men and women.[[50]](#cite_note-WOMEN_ii-53)

Central to Zoroastrianism is the emphasis on moral choice, to choose the responsibility and duty for which one is in the mortal world, or to give up this duty and so facilitate the work of *druj*. Similarly, [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") is rejected in Zoroastrian teaching and the absolute free will of all conscious beings is core, with even divine beings having the ability to choose. Humans bear responsibility for all situations they are in, and in the way they act toward one another. Reward, punishment, happiness, and grief all depend on how individuals live their lives.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54)

In Zoroastrian tradition, life is a temporary state in which a mortal is expected to participate actively in the continuing battle between Asha and Druj. Prior to its incarnation at the birth of the child, the *urvan* (soul) of an individual is still united with its *[fravashi](/wiki/Fravashi "Fravashi")* (personal/higher spirit), which has existed since Ahura Mazda created the universe. Prior to the splitting off of the *urvan*, the fravashi participates in the maintenance of creation led by Ahura Mazda. During the life of a given individual, the fravashi acts as a source of inspiration to perform good actions and as a spiritual protector. The fravashis of ancestors cultural, spiritual, and heroic, associated with illustrious bloodlines, are venerated and can be called upon to aid the living.[[52]](#cite_note-55)

The religion states that active and ethical participation in life through good deeds formed from good thoughts and good words is necessary to ensure happiness and to keep chaos at bay. This active participation is a central element in Zoroaster's concept of [free will](/wiki/Free_will "Free will"), and Zoroastrianism as such rejects extreme forms of [asceticism](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism") and [monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") but historically has allowed for moderate expressions of these concepts.[[47]](#cite_note-DARVĪŠ-50) On the fourth day after death, the urvan is reunited with its fravashi, whereupon the experiences of life in the material world are collected for use in the continuing battle for good in the spiritual world. For the most part, Zoroastrianism does not have a notion of [reincarnation](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation"); albeit Followers of [Ilm-e-Kshnoom](/wiki/Ilm-e-Kshnoom "Ilm-e-Kshnoom") in India, among other currently non-traditional opinions, believe in reincarnation and practice vegetarianism.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce2001205-56)

Zoroastrianism's emphasis on the protection and veneration of nature and its elements has led some to proclaim it as the "world's first proponent of ecology."[[54]](#cite_note-57) The Avesta and other texts call for the protection of [water, earth, fire, and air](/wiki/Classical_element "Classical element") making it, in effect, an ecological religion: "It is not surprising that Mazdaism... is called the first ecological religion. The reverence for Yazatas (divine spirits) emphasizes the preservation of nature" (Avesta: Yasnas 1.19, 3.4, 16.9; Yashts 6.3–4, 10.13).[[55]](#cite_note-58) However, this particular assertion is limited to natural forces held as emanations of asha by the fact that early Zoroastrians had a duty to exterminate "evil" species, a dictate no longer followed in modern Zoroastrianism.[[56]](#cite_note-59) Although there have been various theological statements supporting vegetarianism in Zoroastrianism's history and those who believe that Zoroaster was vegetarian.[[57]](#cite_note-60)

Zoroastrianism is not entirely uniform in theological and philosophical thought, especially with historical and modern influences having a significant impact on individual and local beliefs, practices, values, and vocabulary, sometimes merging with tradition and in other cases displacing it.[[58]](#cite_note-61) The ultimate purpose in the life of a practicing Zoroastrian is to become an *[ashavan](/wiki/Ashavan "Ashavan")* (a master of Asha) and to bring happiness into the world, which contributes to the cosmic battle against evil. The core teachings of Zoroastrianism include:

* Following the threefold path of Asha: *Humata*, *Hūxta*, *Huvarshta* (lit. 'good thoughts, good words, good deeds').[[48]](#cite_note-HUMATA_HŪXTA_HUVARŠTA-51)
* [Practicing charity](/wiki/Charity_(practice) "Charity (practice)") to keep one's soul aligned with Asha and thus with spreading happiness.[[49]](#cite_note-CHARITABLE_FOUNDATIONS-52)
* The spiritual equality and duty of men and women alike.[[50]](#cite_note-WOMEN_ii-53)
* [Being good for the sake of goodness and without the hope of reward](/wiki/Altruism "Altruism") (see *[Ashem Vohu](/wiki/Ashem_Vohu "Ashem Vohu")*).

### Cosmology

Main article: [Zoroastrian cosmology](/wiki/Zoroastrian_cosmology "Zoroastrian cosmology")

#### Cosmogony

According to the Zoroastrian [creation myth](/wiki/Creation_myth "Creation myth"), there is one universal, [transcendent](/wiki/Transcendence_(religion) "Transcendence (religion)"), all-good, and uncreated supreme [creator deity](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity") Ahura Mazda,[[14]](#cite_note-Boyce_1983-17) or the "Wise Lord" (*Ahura* meaning "Lord" and *Mazda* meaning "Wisdom" in [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan")).[[14]](#cite_note-Boyce_1983-17)[[59]](#cite_note-62) [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster") keeps the two attributes separate as two different concepts in most of the [Gathas](/wiki/Gathas "Gathas") yet sometimes combines them into one form. Zoroaster also proclaims that Ahura Mazda is omniscient but not omnipotent.[[14]](#cite_note-Boyce_1983-17) Ahura Mazda existed in light and goodness above, while [Angra Mainyu](/wiki/Angra_Mainyu "Angra Mainyu"), (also referred to in later texts as "Ahriman"),[[60]](#cite_note-AHRIMAN-63)[[61]](#cite_note-64) the destructive spirit/mentality,
existed in darkness and ignorance below. They have existed independently of each other for all time, and manifest contrary substances. In the Gathas, Ahura Mazda is noted as working through emanations known as the Amesha Spenta[[62]](#cite_note-AMƎŠA_SPƎNTA-65) and with the help of "other [ahuras](/wiki/Ahura "Ahura")".[[26]](#cite_note-Hinnells-2007-29) These divine beings called *Amesha Spentas*, support him and are representative and guardians of different aspects of creation and the ideal personality.[[62]](#cite_note-AMƎŠA_SPƎNTA-65) Ahura Mazda is [immanent](/wiki/Immanent "Immanent") in humankind and interacts with creation through these bounteous/holy divinities. In addition to these, He is assisted by a league of countless divinities called [Yazatas](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata"), meaning "worthy of worship." Each Yazata is generally a [hypostasis](/wiki/Hypostasis_(philosophy_and_religion) "Hypostasis (philosophy and religion)") of a moral or physical aspect of creation. Asha[[14]](#cite_note-Boyce_1983-17)[[44]](#cite_note-AṦA-47) is the main spiritual force which comes from Ahura Mazda.[[44]](#cite_note-AṦA-47) It is the cosmic order and is the [antithesis](/wiki/Antithesis "Antithesis") of chaos, which is evident as *druj*, falsehood and disorder, that comes from Angra Mainyu.[[46]](#cite_note-Druj-49)[[32]](#cite_note-Boyce-1983-35) The resulting cosmic conflict involves all of creation, mental/spiritual and material, including humanity at its core, which has an active role to play in the conflict.[[63]](#cite_note-66) The main representative of Asha in this conflict is [Spenta Mainyu](/wiki/Spenta_Mainyu "Spenta Mainyu"), the creative spirit/mentality.[[60]](#cite_note-AHRIMAN-63) Ahura Mazda then created the material and visible world itself in order to ensnare evil. He created the floating, egg-shaped universe in two parts: first the spiritual (*menog*) and 3,000 years later, the physical (*getig*).[[45]](#cite_note-GĒTĪG_AND_MĒNŌG-48) Ahura Mazda then created [Gayomard](/wiki/Gayomard "Gayomard"), the archetypical perfect man, and [Gavaevodata](/wiki/Gavaevodata "Gavaevodata"), the primordial bovine.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54)

While Ahura Mazda created the universe and humankind, Angra Mainyu, whose very nature is to destroy, miscreated demons, evil *daevas*, and noxious creatures (*khrafstar*) such as snakes, ants, and flies. Angra Mainyu created an opposite, evil being for each good being, except for humans, which he found he could not match. Angra Mainyu invaded the universe through the base of the sky, inflicting Gayomard and the bull with suffering and death. However, the evil forces were trapped in the universe and could not retreat. The dying primordial man and bovine emitted seeds, which were protected by [Mah](/wiki/Mah "Mah"), the Moon. From the bull's seed grew all beneficial plants and animals of the world and from the man's seed grew a plant whose leaves became the [first human couple](/wiki/Mashya_and_Mashyana "Mashya and Mashyana"). Humans thus struggle in a two-fold universe of the material and spiritual trapped and in long combat with evil. The evils of this physical world are not products of an inherent weakness but are the fault of Angra Mainyu's assault on creation. This assault turned the perfectly flat, peaceful, and daily illuminated world into a mountainous, violent place that is half night.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54) According to Zoroastrian [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), in articulating the [Ahuna Vairya](/wiki/Ahuna_Vairya "Ahuna Vairya") formula, Ahura Mazda made the ultimate triumph of good against Angra Mainyu evident.[[64]](#cite_note-AHUNWAR-67) Ahura Mazda will ultimately prevail over the evil [Angra Mainyu](/wiki/Angra_Mainyu "Angra Mainyu"), at which point reality will undergo a cosmic renovation called [Frashokereti](/wiki/Frashokereti "Frashokereti")[[65]](#cite_note-68) and limited time will end. In the final renovation, all of creation—even the souls of the dead that were initially banished to or chose to descend into "darkness"—will be reunited with Ahura Mazda in the [Kshatra Vairya](/wiki/Kshatra_Vairya "Kshatra Vairya") (meaning "best dominion"),[[66]](#cite_note-69) being resurrected to immortality.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Cosmography

Zoroastrian [cosmography](/wiki/Cosmography "Cosmography"), which refers to the description of the structure of the cosmos in Zoroastrian literature and theology, involves a primary division of the cosmos into heaven and earth.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanaino201958-70) The heaven is composed of three parts: the lower-most part, which is where the fixed stars may be found; the middle part, where the domain of the moon is located, and the upper part, which is the domain of the sun and unreachable by Ahirman.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanaino20199-71) Further above the highest level of the heaven/sky includes regions described as the Endless Lights, as well as the Thrones of Amahraspandān and Ohrmazd.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanaino201985-72) Although this is the basic framework which occurs in Avestan texts, later Zoroastrian literature would elaborate on this picture by further subdividing the lowest part of heaven to achieve a total of six or seven layers.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanaino1995205–208-73) The Earth itself was described as possessing three primary mountains: Mount Hukairiia, whose peak was the focal point of the revolution of the star Sadwēs; Mount Haraitī, whose peak was the focal point of the revolution of the sun and the moon, and the greatest of them all, the Harā Bərəz whose peak was located at the center of the Earth and which was the first in a chain of 2,244 mountains which, together, encircled the Earth.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanaino201972–73-74) Although the planets are not described in early Zoroastrian sources, they entered Zoroastrian thought in the Middle Persian period: they were demonized and took on the names *Anāhīd* (Pahlavi for [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus")), *Tīr* ([Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)")), *Wahrām* ([Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars")), *Ohrmazd* ([Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")), and *Kēwān* ([Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn")).[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanaino2015238–240-75)

### Eschatology

Main article: [Frashokereti](/wiki/Frashokereti "Frashokereti")

Individual judgment at death is at the [Chinvat Bridge](/wiki/Chinvat_Bridge "Chinvat Bridge") ("bridge of judgement" or "bridge of choice"), which each human must cross, facing a spiritual judgment, though modern belief is split as to whether it is representative of a mental decision during life to choose between good and evil or an afterworld location. Humans' actions under their free will through choice determine the outcome. According to tradition, the soul is judged by the Yazatas [Mithra](/wiki/Mithra "Mithra"), [Sraosha](/wiki/Sraosha "Sraosha"), and [Rashnu](/wiki/Rashnu "Rashnu"), where depending on the verdict one is either greeted at the bridge by a beautiful, sweet-smelling maiden or by an ugly, foul-smelling old hag representing their [Daena](/wiki/Daena "Daena") affected by their actions in life. The maiden leads the dead safely across the bridge, which widens and becomes pleasant for the righteous, towards the House of Song. The hag leads the dead down a bridge that narrows to a razor's edge and is full of stench until the departed falls off into the abyss towards the House of Lies.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54)[[73]](#cite_note-76) Those with a balance of good and evil go to [Hamistagan](/wiki/Hamistagan "Hamistagan"), a purgatorial realm mentioned in the 9th century work *[Dadestan-i Denig](/wiki/Dadestan-i_Denig "Dadestan-i Denig")*.[[74]](#cite_note-77)

The House of Lies is considered temporary and reformative; punishments fit the crimes, and souls do not rest in eternal damnation. Hell contains foul smells and evil food, a smothering darkness, and souls are packed tightly together although they believe they are in total isolation.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54)

In ancient [Zoroastrian eschatology](/wiki/Zoroastrian_eschatology "Zoroastrian eschatology"), a 3,000-year struggle between good and evil will be fought, punctuated by evil's final assault. During the final assault, the sun and moon will darken, and humankind will lose its reverence for religion, family, and elders. The world will fall into winter, and Angra Mainyu's most fearsome miscreant, [Azi Dahaka](/wiki/Azi_Dahaka "Azi Dahaka"), will break free and terrorize the world.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54)

According to legend, the final savior of the world, known as the [Saoshyant](/wiki/Saoshyant "Saoshyant"), will be born to a virgin impregnated by the seed of Zoroaster while bathing in a lake. The Saoshyant will raise the dead—including those in all afterworlds—for final judgment, returning the wicked to hell to be purged of bodily sin. Next, all will wade through a river of molten metal in which the righteous will not burn but through which the impure will be completely purified. The forces of good will ultimately triumph over evil, rendering it forever impotent but not destroyed. The Saoshyant and Ahura Mazda will offer a bull as a final sacrifice for all time and all humans will become immortal. Mountains will again flatten and valleys will rise; the House of Song will descend to the moon, and the earth will rise to meet them both.[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54) Humanity will require two judgments because there are as many aspects to our being: spiritual (*menog*) and physical (*getig*).[[51]](#cite_note-Cavendish-1980-54)

## Practices and rituals

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Dinastia_tang%2C_shanxi%2C_straniero_dal_volto_velato%2C_600-750_ca.JPG/330px-Dinastia_tang%2C_shanxi%2C_straniero_dal_volto_velato%2C_600-750_ca.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dinastia_tang,_shanxi,_straniero_dal_volto_velato,_600-750_ca.JPG)

An 8th century [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") [Chinese clay figurine](/wiki/Chinese_ceramics "Chinese ceramics") of a [Sogdian](/wiki/Sogdia "Sogdia") man wearing a distinctive cap and face veil, possibly a camel rider or even a Zoroastrian priest engaging in a ritual at a [fire temple](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple"), since face veils were used to avoid contaminating the holy fire with breath or saliva; [Museum of Oriental Art (Turin)](/wiki/Museum_of_Oriental_Art_(Turin) "Museum of Oriental Art (Turin)"), Italy.[[75]](#cite_note-78)

Throughout Zoroastrian history, shrines and [temples](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple") have been the focus of worship and pilgrimage for adherents of the religion. Early Zoroastrians were recorded as worshiping in the 5th century BCE on mounds and hills where fires were lit below the open skies.[[76]](#cite_note-79) In the wake of Achaemenid expansion, shrines were constructed throughout the empire and particularly influenced the role of [Mithra](/wiki/Mithra "Mithra"), [Aredvi Sura Anahita](/wiki/Aredvi_Sura_Anahita "Aredvi Sura Anahita"), [Verethragna](/wiki/Verethragna "Verethragna") and [Tishtrya](/wiki/Tishtrya "Tishtrya"), alongside other traditional Yazata who all have hymns within the Avesta and also local deities and culture-heroes. Today, enclosed and covered fire temples tend to be the focus of community worship where fires of varying grades are maintained by the clergy assigned to the temples.[[77]](#cite_note-80)

The incorporation of cultural and local rituals is quite common and traditions have been passed down in historically Zoroastrian communities such as herbal healing practices, wedding ceremonies, and the like.[[78]](#cite_note-81)[[79]](#cite_note-82)[[43]](#cite_note-ZOROASTRIAN_RITUALS-46) Traditionally, Zoroastrian rituals have also included [shamanic](/wiki/Shamanic "Shamanic") elements involving [mystical](/wiki/Mystical "Mystical") methods such as [spirit travel](/wiki/Spirit_travel "Spirit travel") to the invisible realm and involving the consumption of [fortified wine](/wiki/Fortified_wine "Fortified wine"), [Haoma](/wiki/Haoma "Haoma"), [mang](/wiki/Bhang "Bhang"), and other ritual aids.[[80]](#cite_note-83)[[45]](#cite_note-GĒTĪG_AND_MĒNŌG-48)[[81]](#cite_note-84)[[82]](#cite_note-85)[[83]](#cite_note-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Ossuary%2C_Mullakurgan_%28near_Samarkand%29%2C_Uzbekistan%2C_7-8th_century_CE.jpg/250px-Ossuary%2C_Mullakurgan_%28near_Samarkand%29%2C_Uzbekistan%2C_7-8th_century_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ossuary,_Mullakurgan_(near_Samarkand),_Uzbekistan,_7-8th_century_CE.jpg)

[Ossuary](/wiki/Ossuary "Ossuary") with reliefs of Zoroastrian priests attending a fire, Mullakurgan (near Samarkand), Uzbekistan, 7–8th century CE.[[84]](#cite_note-87)

According to former South Asian studies professor Bettina Robotika, purification ceremonies associated with water (*[aban](/wiki/Aban "Aban")*) and fire (*[atar](/wiki/Atar "Atar")*) are considered the basis of Zoroastrian ritual life. Some Zoroastrian communities practice [ritual exposure](/wiki/Excarnation "Excarnation"), mainly in regions of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") where the practice is legal and there are healthy populations of scavenger birds. A corpse is considered a host for decay or *[druj](/wiki/Asha "Asha")*, and scripture enjoins that it be disposed in a manner that does not pollute the good creation. Structures called [Towers of Silence](/wiki/Towers_of_Silence "Towers of Silence") are commonly identified with this practice.[[85]](#cite_note-88)

The central ritual of Zoroastrianism is the [Yasna](/wiki/Yasna "Yasna"), which is a recitation of the eponymous book of the Avesta and sacrificial ritual ceremony involving [Haoma](/wiki/Haoma "Haoma").[[86]](#cite_note-YASNA-89) Extensions to the Yasna ritual are possible through use of the [Visperad](/wiki/Visperad "Visperad") and [Vendidad](/wiki/Vendidad "Vendidad"), but such an extended ritual is rare in modern Zoroastrianism.[[87]](#cite_note-90)[[88]](#cite_note-91) The Yasna itself descended from [Indo-Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") sacrificial ceremonies and animal sacrifice of varying degrees are mentioned in the Avesta and are still practiced in Zoroastrianism albeit through reduced forms such as the sacrifice of fat before meals.[[89]](#cite_note-SACRIFICE_i-92) High rituals such as the Yasna are considered to be the purview of the [Mobads](/wiki/Mobad "Mobad") with a corpus of individual and communal rituals and prayers included in the [Khordeh Avesta](/wiki/Khordeh_Avesta "Khordeh Avesta").[[86]](#cite_note-YASNA-89)[[90]](#cite_note-93)

A Zoroastrian is welcomed into the faith through the [Navjote](/wiki/Navjote "Navjote")/Sedreh Pushi ceremony, which is traditionally conducted during the later childhood or pre-teen years of the aspirant, though there is no defined age limit for the ritual.[[43]](#cite_note-ZOROASTRIAN_RITUALS-46)[[91]](#cite_note-Avesta-94) After the ceremony, Zoroastrians are encouraged to wear their *[sedreh](/wiki/Sedreh "Sedreh")* (ritual shirt) and *[kushti](/wiki/Kushti "Kushti")* (ritual girdle) daily as a spiritual reminder and for mystical protection, though reformist Zoroastrians tend to only wear them during festivals, ceremonies, and prayers.[[92]](#cite_note-95)[[43]](#cite_note-ZOROASTRIAN_RITUALS-46)[[91]](#cite_note-Avesta-94)

Historically, Zoroastrians are encouraged to pray the five daily [Gāhs](/wiki/G%C4%81h "Gāh") and to maintain and celebrate the various holy festivals of the [Zoroastrian calendar](/wiki/Zoroastrian_calendar "Zoroastrian calendar"), which can differ from community to community.[[93]](#cite_note-96)[[94]](#cite_note-97) Zoroastrian prayers, called *[manthras](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra")*, are conducted usually with hands outstretched in imitation of Zoroaster's prayer style described in the Gathas and are of a reflectionary and supplicant nature believed to be endowed with the ability to banish evil.[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99)[[64]](#cite_note-AHUNWAR-67) Devout Zoroastrians are known to cover their heads during prayer, either with traditional [topi](/wiki/Kufi "Kufi"), scarves, other headwear, or even just their hands. However, full coverage and veiling which is traditional in Islamic practice is not a part of Zoroastrianism and Zoroastrian women in Iran wear their head coverings displaying hair and their faces to defy mandates by the [Islamic Republic of Iran](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Iran "Islamic Republic of Iran").[[97]](#cite_note-100)

## Scripture

See also: [Zoroastrian literature](/wiki/Zoroastrian_literature "Zoroastrian literature")

### *Avesta*

Main articles: [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") and [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan")

The *Avesta* is a collection of the central religious texts of Zoroastrianism written in the old Iranian dialect of [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan"). The history of the Avesta is speculated upon in many [Pahlavi texts](/wiki/Pahlavi_scripts "Pahlavi scripts") with varying degrees of authority, with the current version of the Avesta dating at oldest from the times of the Sasanian Empire.[[98]](#cite_note-AVESTA_i-101) The Avesta was "composed at different times, providing a series of snapshots of the religion that allow historians to see how it changed over time".[[99]](#cite_note-102) According to Middle Persian tradition, Ahura Mazda created the 21 Nasks of the original Avesta which Zoroaster brought to [Vishtaspa](/wiki/Vishtaspa "Vishtaspa"). Here, two copies were created, one which was put in the house of archives and the other put in the Imperial treasury. During Alexander's conquest of Persia, the Avesta (written on 1,200 ox-hides) was burned, and the scientific sections that the Greeks could use were dispersed among themselves. However, there is no strong historical evidence for this and they remain contested despite affirmations from the Zoroastrian tradition, whether it be the *[Denkart](/wiki/Denkard "Denkard")*, *Tansar-nāma*, *[Ardāy Wirāz Nāmag](/wiki/Book_of_Arda_Viraf "Book of Arda Viraf")*, *[Bundahsin](/wiki/Bundahishn "Bundahishn")*, *[Zand-i Wahman yasn](/wiki/Zand-i_Wahman_yasn "Zand-i Wahman yasn")* or the transmitted oral tradition.[[98]](#cite_note-AVESTA_i-101)[[100]](#cite_note-103)

As tradition continues, under the reign of King Valax (identified with a [Vologases](/wiki/Vologases_I_of_Parthia "Vologases I of Parthia") of the [Arsacid dynasty](/wiki/Arsacid_dynasty "Arsacid dynasty")[[101]](#cite_note-104)), an attempt was made to restore what was considered the Avesta. During the [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire"), Ardeshir ordered Tansar, [his high priest](/wiki/Dastur "Dastur"), to finish the work that King Valax had started. [Shapur I](/wiki/Shapur_I "Shapur I") sent priests to locate the scientific text portions of the Avesta that were in the possession of the Greeks.[[102]](#cite_note-105) Under [Shapur II](/wiki/Shapur_II "Shapur II"), Arderbad Mahrespandand revised the canon to ensure its orthodox character, while under [Khosrow I](/wiki/Khosrow_I "Khosrow I"), the Avesta was translated into [Pahlavi](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian").

The compilation of the Avesta can be authoritatively traced, however, to the Sasanian Empire, of which only fraction survive today if the [Middle Persian literature](/wiki/Middle_Persian_literature "Middle Persian literature") is correct.[[98]](#cite_note-AVESTA_i-101) The later manuscripts all date from after the fall of the Sasanian Empire, the latest being from 1288, 590 years after the fall of the Sasanian Empire. The texts that remain today are the [Gathas](/wiki/Gathas "Gathas"), [Yasna](/wiki/Yasna "Yasna"), [Visperad](/wiki/Visperad "Visperad") and the [Vendidad](/wiki/Vendidad "Vendidad"), of which the latter's inclusion is disputed within the faith.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Along with these texts is the individual, communal, and ceremonial prayer book called the [Khordeh Avesta](/wiki/Khordeh_Avesta "Khordeh Avesta"), which contains the [Yashts](/wiki/Yasht "Yasht") and other important hymns, prayers, and rituals. The rest of the materials from the Avesta are called "Avestan fragments" in that they are written in Avestan, incomplete, and generally of unknown provenance.[[104]](#cite_note-107)

### Middle Persian (Pahlavi)

Middle Persian and Pahlavi works created in the 9th and 10th century contain many religious Zoroastrian books, as most of the writers and copyists were part of the Zoroastrian clergy. The most significant and important books of this era include the [Denkard](/wiki/Denkard "Denkard"), [Bundahishn](/wiki/Bundahishn "Bundahishn"), [Menog-i Khrad](/wiki/Menog-i_Khrad "Menog-i Khrad"), [Selections of Zadspram](/wiki/Wizidagiha-i_Zadspram "Wizidagiha-i Zadspram"), [Jamasp Namag](/wiki/Jamasp_Namag "Jamasp Namag"), Epistles of Manucher, [Rivayats](/wiki/Rivayat "Rivayat"), Dadestan-i-Denig, and [Arda Viraf Namag](/wiki/Arda_Viraf_Namag "Arda Viraf Namag"). The scriptural status and authority of Middle Persian religious texts are disputed among Zoroastrians,[[105]](#cite_note-108)[[106]](#cite_note-109)[[107]](#cite_note-110) as John Hinnells points out that “most Iranians stress the authority of revealed scripture, the *Gāthā*s, rather than the later Pahlavi texts which ‘orthodox’ Parsis value.”[[108]](#cite_note-111)

## History

### Zoroaster

Main article: [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster")

Zoroastrianism was founded by Zoroaster in ancient Iran. The precise date of the founding of the religion is uncertain and estimates vary wildly from 2000 BCE to "200 years before Alexander". Zoroaster was born – in either Northeast Iran or Southwest Afghanistan – into a culture with a [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheistic "Polytheistic") religion, which featured excessive [animal sacrifice](/wiki/Animal_sacrifice "Animal sacrifice")[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce200126-112) and the excessive ritual use of intoxicants. His life was influenced profoundly by the attempts of his people to find peace and stability in the face of constant threats of raiding and conflict. Zoroaster's birth and early life are little documented but speculated upon heavily in later texts. What is known is recorded in the [Gathas](/wiki/Gathas "Gathas"), forming the core of the Avesta, which contain hymns thought to have been composed by Zoroaster himself. Born into the [Spitama](/wiki/Spitama "Spitama") clan, he refers to himself as a poet-priest and [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet"). He had a wife, three sons, and three daughters, the numbers of which are gathered from various texts.[[110]](#cite_note-113)

Zoroaster rejected many of the gods of the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") Iranians and their oppressive [class structure](/wiki/Caste_system "Caste system"), in which the Kavis and Karapans (princes and priests) controlled the ordinary people. He also opposed cruel animal sacrifices and the excessive use of the possibly [hallucinogenic](/wiki/Hallucinogenic "Hallucinogenic") [Haoma](/wiki/Haoma "Haoma") plant (conjectured to have been a species of [ephedra](/wiki/Ephedra_(genus) "Ephedra (genus)") or *[Peganum harmala](/wiki/Peganum_harmala "Peganum harmala")*), but did not condemn either practice outright, providing moderation was observed.[[89]](#cite_note-SACRIFICE_i-92)[[111]](#cite_note-114)

### Legendary accounts

According to later Zoroastrian tradition, when Zoroaster was 30 years old, he went into the Daiti river to draw water for a [Haoma](/wiki/Haoma "Haoma") ceremony; when he emerged, he received a vision of [Vohu Manah](/wiki/Vohu_Manah "Vohu Manah"). After this, Vohu Manah took him to the other six Amesha Spentas, where he received the completion of his vision.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce200119-115) This vision radically transformed his view of the world, and he tried to teach this view to others. Zoroaster believed in one supreme creator deity and acknowledged this creator's emanations ([Amesha Spenta](/wiki/Amesha_Spenta "Amesha Spenta")) and other divinities which he called Ahuras ([Yazata](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata")).[[31]](#cite_note-AHURA-34) Some of the deities of the old religion, the *[Daevas](/wiki/Daevas "Daevas")* (etymologically similar to the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") *[Devas](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)")*), appeared to delight in war and strife and were condemned as evil workers of Angra Mainyu by Zoroaster.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

Zoroaster's ideas were not taken up quickly; he originally only had one convert: his cousin Maidhyoimanha.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce200130–31-117)

#### Cypress of Kashmar

Main article: [Cypress of Kashmar](/wiki/Cypress_of_Kashmar "Cypress of Kashmar")

The Cypress of Kashmar is a mythical cypress tree of legendary beauty and gargantuan dimensions. It is said to have sprung from a branch brought by [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster") from Paradise and to have stood in today's [Kashmar](/wiki/Kashmar "Kashmar") in northeastern Iran and to have been planted by Zoroaster in honor of the conversion of [King Vishtaspa](/wiki/Vishtaspa "Vishtaspa") to Zoroastrianism. According to the Iranian physicist and historian [Zakariya al-Qazwini](/wiki/Zakariya_al-Qazwini "Zakariya al-Qazwini") King Vishtaspa had been a patron of Zoroaster who planted the tree himself. In his *[ʿAjā'ib al-makhlūqāt wa gharā'ib al-mawjūdāt](/wiki/%CA%BFAj%C4%81%27ib_al-makhl%C5%ABq%C4%81t_wa_ghar%C4%81%27ib_al-mawj%C5%ABd%C4%81t "ʿAjā'ib al-makhlūqāt wa gharā'ib al-mawjūdāt")*, he further describes how the [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") caliph [Al-Mutawakkil](/wiki/Al-Mutawakkil "Al-Mutawakkil") in 247 AH (861 CE) caused the mighty cypress to be felled, and then transported it across Iran, to be used for beams in his new palace at [Samarra](/wiki/Samarra "Samarra"). Before, he wanted the tree to be reconstructed before his eyes. This was done in spite of protests by the Iranians, who offered a very great sum of money to save the tree. Al-Mutawakkil never saw the cypress, because he was murdered by a [Turkish](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") soldier (possibly in the employ of his son) on the night when it arrived on the banks of the Tigris.[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119)

#### Fire Temple of Kashmar

Kashmar Fire Temple was the first Zoroastrian fire temple built by [Vishtaspa](/wiki/Vishtaspa "Vishtaspa") at the request of Zoroaster in Kashmar. In a part of [Ferdowsi](/wiki/Ferdowsi "Ferdowsi")'s [Shahnameh](/wiki/Shahnameh "Shahnameh"), the story of finding Zarathustra and accepting Vishtaspa's religion is regulated that after accepting Zoroastrian religion, Vishtaspa sends priests all over the universe And Azar enters the fire temples (domes) and the first of them is [Adur Burzen-Mihr](/wiki/Adur_Burzen-Mihr "Adur Burzen-Mihr") who founded in Kashmar and planted a cypress tree in front of the fire temple and made it a symbol of accepting the Bahi religion And he sent priests all over the world, and commanded all the famous men and women to come to that place of worship.[[117]](#cite_note-120)

According to the [Paikuli inscription](/wiki/Paikuli_inscription "Paikuli inscription"), during the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), Kashmar was part of [Greater Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan"), and the Sasanians worked hard to revive the ancient religion. It still remains a few kilometers above the ancient city of Kashmar in the [castle complex of Atashgah](/wiki/Atashgah_Castle "Atashgah Castle").[[118]](#cite_note-121)

### Early history

See also: [Avestan period](/wiki/Avestan_period "Avestan period") and [Airyanem Vaejah](/wiki/Airyanem_Vaejah "Airyanem Vaejah")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Young_avestan_geography.png/250px-Young_avestan_geography.png)](/wiki/File:Young_avestan_geography.png)

Geographical horizon of the Avestan people during the Young Avestan period. Sources for the different localizations are in the file description.

The roots of Zoroastrianism are thought to lie in a common prehistoric [Indo-Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") religious system dating back to the early 2nd millennium BCE.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoltz201310–18-122) The prophet Zoroaster himself, though traditionally dated to the 6th century BCE,[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[13]](#cite_note-Schmitt-2002-16)[[121]](#cite_note-124) is thought by many modern historians to have been a reformer of the polytheistic Iranian religion who lived much earlier during the second half of the second millennium BCE.[[122]](#cite_note-125)[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-127)[[125]](#cite_note-Atlas-128) Zoroastrian tradition names [Airyanem Vaejah](/wiki/Airyanem_Vaejah "Airyanem Vaejah") as the home of Zarathustra and the birthplace of the religion. No consensus exists as to the localization of Airyanem Vaejah, but the region of [Khwarezm](/wiki/Khwarezm "Khwarezm") has been considered by modern scholars as a candidate.[[126]](#cite_note-129) Zoroastrianism as a religion was not firmly established until centuries later during the [Young Avestan period](/wiki/Avestan_period "Avestan period"). At this time, the Zoroastrian community was concentrated in the eastern portion of [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran").[[127]](#cite_note-130) Although no consensus exists on the chronology of the Avestan period, the lack of any discernable [Persian](/wiki/Achaemenid_empire "Achaemenid empire") and [Median](/wiki/Median_empire "Median empire") influence in the [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") makes a time frame in the first half of the first millennium BCE likely.[[128]](#cite_note-131)

### Classical antiquity

See also: [Western references to Zoroaster and Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroaster#Western_references_to_Zoroaster_and_Zoroastrianism "Zoroaster") and [Religion in the Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Achaemenid_Empire "Religion in the Achaemenid Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Head_of_Bactrian_ruler_%28Satrap%29%2C_Temple_of_the_Oxus%2C_Takht-i-Sangin%2C_3rd-2nd_century_BC.jpg/250px-Head_of_Bactrian_ruler_%28Satrap%29%2C_Temple_of_the_Oxus%2C_Takht-i-Sangin%2C_3rd-2nd_century_BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Head_of_Bactrian_ruler_(Satrap),_Temple_of_the_Oxus,_Takht-i-Sangin,_3rd-2nd_century_BC.jpg)

Painted clay and [alabaster](/wiki/Alabaster "Alabaster") head of a Zoroastrian priest wearing a distinctive [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria")-style headdress, [Takhti-Sangin](/wiki/Takhti-Sangin "Takhti-Sangin"), [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [Greco-Bactrian kingdom](/wiki/Greco-Bactrian_kingdom "Greco-Bactrian kingdom"), 3rd–2nd century BCE.[[129]](#cite_note-132)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Pasargad_Tomb_Cyrus3.jpg/250px-Pasargad_Tomb_Cyrus3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pasargad_Tomb_Cyrus3.jpg)

The [Tomb of Cyrus the Great](/wiki/Tomb_of_Cyrus "Tomb of Cyrus") at [Pasargadae](/wiki/Pasargadae "Pasargadae"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")

Zoroastrianism enters recorded history in the mid-5th century BCE. [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus")' *[The Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)")* (completed c. 440 BCE) includes a description of [Greater Iranian](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran") society with what may be recognizably Zoroastrian features, including exposure of the dead.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

*The Histories* is a primary source of information on the early period of the [Achaemenid era](/wiki/Achaemenid_era "Achaemenid era") (648–330 BCE), in particular with respect to the role of the [Magi](/wiki/Magi "Magi"). According to Herodotus, the Magi were the sixth tribe of the [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes") (until the unification of the Persian empire under [Cyrus the Great](/wiki/Cyrus_the_Great "Cyrus the Great"), all Iranians were referred to as "Mede" or "Mada" by the peoples of the Ancient World) and wielded considerable influence at the courts of the Median emperors.[[131]](#cite_note-Perseus_Digital_Library-134)

Following the unification of the Median and Persian empires in 550 BCE, Cyrus the Great and later his son [Cambyses II](/wiki/Cambyses_II "Cambyses II") curtailed the powers of the Magi after they had attempted to sow dissent following their loss of influence. In 522 BCE, the Magi revolted and set up a rival claimant to the throne. The usurper, pretending to be Cyrus' younger son [Smerdis](/wiki/Smerdis "Smerdis"), took power shortly thereafter.[[132]](#cite_note-135) Owing to the [despotic rule](/wiki/Despotism "Despotism") of Cambyses and his long absence in Egypt, "the whole people, Persians, Medes and all the other nations" acknowledged the usurper, especially as he granted a remission of taxes for three years.[[131]](#cite_note-Perseus_Digital_Library-134)

Darius I and later [Achaemenid emperors](/wiki/Achaemenid_dynasty "Achaemenid dynasty") acknowledged their devotion to Ahura Mazda in inscriptions, as attested to several times in the [Behistun](/wiki/Behistun "Behistun") inscription and appear to have continued the model of coexistence with other religions. Whether Darius was a follower of the teachings of Zoroaster has not been conclusively established as there is no indication of note that worship of Ahura Mazda was exclusively a Zoroastrian practice.[[133]](#cite_note-136)

According to later Zoroastrian legend (*[Denkard](/wiki/Denkard "Denkard")* and the *[Book of Arda Viraf](/wiki/Book_of_Arda_Viraf "Book of Arda Viraf")*), many sacred texts were lost when [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great")'s troops invaded [Persepolis](/wiki/Persepolis "Persepolis") and subsequently destroyed the royal library there. [Diodorus Siculus](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus")'s *Bibliotheca historica*, which was completed c. 60 BCE, appears to substantiate this Zoroastrian legend.[[134]](#cite_note-137) According to one archaeological examination, the ruins of the palace of [Xerxes I](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I") bear traces of having been burned.[[135]](#cite_note-138) Whether a vast collection of (semi-)religious texts "written on parchment in gold ink", as suggested by the *Denkard*, actually existed remains a matter of speculation.[[136]](#cite_note-139)

Alexander's conquests largely displaced Zoroastrianism with [Hellenistic beliefs](/wiki/Hellenistic_religion "Hellenistic religion"),[[125]](#cite_note-Atlas-128) though the religion continued to be practiced many centuries following the demise of the Achaemenids in mainland Persia and the core regions of the former Achaemenid Empire, most notably [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"), [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"). In the [Cappadocian kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cappadocia "Kingdom of Cappadocia"), whose territory was formerly an Achaemenid possession, Persian colonists, cut off from their co-religionists in Iran proper, continued to practice the faith [Zoroastrianism] of their forefathers; and there [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), observing in the first century BCE, records (XV.3.15) that these "fire kindlers" possessed many "holy places of the Persian Gods", as well as fire temples.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce200185-140) Strabo further states that these were "noteworthy enclosures; and in their midst there is an altar, on which there is a large quantity of ashes and where the magi keep the [fire ever burning](/wiki/Fire_ever_burning "Fire ever burning")."[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce200185-140) It was not until the end of the [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire") (247 BCE – 224 CE) that Zoroastrianism would receive renewed interest.[[125]](#cite_note-Atlas-128)

### Late antiquity

See also: [Religion in the Parthian era](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Parthian_era "Religion in the Parthian era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Zoroastrian_ossuary%2C_7th-8th_century_CE%2C_Hirman_Tepe%2C_Uzbekistan.jpg/250px-Zoroastrian_ossuary%2C_7th-8th_century_CE%2C_Hirman_Tepe%2C_Uzbekistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zoroastrian_ossuary,_7th-8th_century_CE,_Hirman_Tepe,_Uzbekistan.jpg)

A Zoroastrian [ossuary](/wiki/Ossuary "Ossuary"), 7–8th century CE, Hirman Tepe, Uzbekistan.[[138]](#cite_note-141)

As late as the [Parthian](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire") period, [a form of Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Armenian_mythology "Armenian mythology") was without a doubt the dominant religion in the [Armenian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Armenia_(antiquity) "Kingdom of Armenia (antiquity)") lands.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce200184-142) The [Sassanids](/wiki/Sassanids "Sassanids") aggressively promoted the [Zurvanite](/wiki/Zurvanite "Zurvanite") form of Zoroastrianism, often building fire temples in captured territories to promote the religion. During the period of their centuries-long [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") over the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), the Sassanids made attempts to promote Zoroastrianism there with considerable successes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Due to its ties to the Christian [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), Persia's archrival since Parthian times, the Sassanids were suspicious of [Roman Christianity](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire"), and after the reign of [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great"), sometimes persecuted it.[[140]](#cite_note-143) In 451 CE, the Sassanid authority clashed with their [Armenian](/wiki/Persian_Armenia "Persian Armenia") subjects in the [Battle of Avarayr](/wiki/Battle_of_Avarayr "Battle of Avarayr"), making them officially break with the Roman Church. But the Sassanids tolerated or even sometimes favored the Christianity of the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East"). The acceptance of Christianity in Georgia ([Caucasian Iberia](/wiki/Caucasian_Iberia "Caucasian Iberia")) saw the Zoroastrian religion there slowly but surely decline,[[141]](#cite_note-144) but as late the 5th century CE, it was still widely practised as something like a second established religion.[[142]](#cite_note-145)[[143]](#cite_note-146) The [Sasanians](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire") enforced a caste system in line with Zoroastrian doctrine. These were:

1. *Asronan* (priests)
2. *Arteshtaran* (warriors)
3. *Wastaryoshan* (commoners)
4. *Hutukhshan* (artisans)[[144]](#cite_note-147)[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaryaee200843–47-148)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaki1992652–658-149)

### Decline in the Middle Ages

See also: [Persecution of Zoroastrians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Zoroastrians "Persecution of Zoroastrians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Hamza_Burns_Zarthust%E2%80%99s_Chest_and_Shatters_the_Urn_with_his_Ashes.jpg/250px-Hamza_Burns_Zarthust%E2%80%99s_Chest_and_Shatters_the_Urn_with_his_Ashes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hamza_Burns_Zarthust%E2%80%99s_Chest_and_Shatters_the_Urn_with_his_Ashes.jpg)

A scene from the [Hamzanama](/wiki/Hamzanama "Hamzanama") where [Hamza ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib](/wiki/Hamza_ibn_%27Abd_al-Muttalib "Hamza ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib") Burns Zarthust's Chest and Shatters the Urn with his Ashes

Over the course of 22 years during the 7th century, most of the Sasanian Empire was [conquered by the emerging Muslim caliphate](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia").[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHourani194787-150) Although the administration of the state was rapidly Islamicized and subsumed under the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate"), in the beginning "there was little serious pressure" exerted on newly subjected people to adopt Islam.[[148]](#cite_note-151) Because of their sheer numbers, the conquered Zoroastrians had to be treated as *[dhimmis](/wiki/Dhimmi "Dhimmi")* (despite doubts of the validity of this identification that persisted down the centuries),[[149]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-152) which made them eligible for protection. Islamic jurists took the stance that only Muslims could be perfectly moral, but "unbelievers might as well be left to their iniquities, so long as these did not vex their overlords."[[149]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-152) In the main, once the conquest was over and "local terms were agreed on", the Arab governors protected the local populations in exchange for tribute.[[149]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-152)

The Arabs adopted the Sasanian tax-system, both the land-tax levied on landowners and the [poll-tax](/wiki/Poll-tax "Poll-tax") levied on individuals,[[149]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-152) called *[jizya](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya")*, a tax levied on non-Muslims (i.e., the *dhimmis*). In time, this poll-tax came to be used as a means to humble the non-Muslims, and a number of laws and restrictions evolved to emphasize their inferior status. Under the early orthodox [caliphs](/wiki/Caliphs "Caliphs"), as long as the non-Muslims paid their taxes and adhered to the *dhimmi* laws, administrators were enjoined to leave non-Muslims "in their religion and their land".[[150]](#cite_note-153)

Under [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") rule, Muslim Iranians (who by then were in the majority) in many instances showed severe disregard for and mistreated local Zoroastrians. For example, in the 9th century, a deeply venerated [cypress tree](/wiki/Cypress_tree "Cypress tree") in [Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan") (which Parthian-era legend supposed had been planted by Zoroaster himself) was felled for the construction of a palace in Baghdad, 2,000 miles (3,200 km) away. In the 10th century, on the day that a [Tower of Silence](/wiki/Tower_of_Silence "Tower of Silence") had been completed at much trouble and expense, a Muslim official contrived to get up onto it, and to call the *[adhan](/wiki/Adhan "Adhan")* (the Muslim call to prayer) from its walls. This was turned into a pretext to annex the building.[[151]](#cite_note-154)

Ultimately, Muslim scholars like [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") found few records left of the belief of for instance the [Khawarizmians](/wiki/Khwarazmian_dynasty "Khwarazmian dynasty") because figures like [Qutayba ibn Muslim](/wiki/Qutayba_ibn_Muslim "Qutayba ibn Muslim") "extinguished and ruined in every possible way all those who knew how to write and read the Khawarizmi writing, who knew the history of the country and who studied their sciences." As a result, "these things are involved in so much obscurity that it is impossible to obtain an accurate knowledge of the history of the country since the time of Islam..."[[152]](#cite_note-155)

#### Conversion

Though subject to a new leadership and harassment, the Zoroastrians were able to continue their former ways, although there was a slow but steady social and economic pressure to convert,[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-2-157) with the nobility and city-dwellers being the first to do so, while Islam was accepted more slowly among the peasantry and landed gentry.[[155]](#cite_note-158) "Power and worldly-advantage" now lay with followers of Islam, and although the "official policy was one of aloof contempt, there were individual Muslims eager to [proselytize](/wiki/Proselytize "Proselytize") and ready to use all sorts of means to do so."[[154]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-2-157)

In time, a tradition evolved by which Islam was made to appear as a partly Iranian religion. One example of this was a legend that [Husayn](/wiki/Husayn_ibn_Ali "Husayn ibn Ali"), son of the fourth caliph [Ali](/wiki/Ali "Ali") and grandson of Islam's prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad"), had married a captive Sassanid princess named [Shahrbanu](/wiki/Shahrbanu "Shahrbanu"). This "wholly fictitious figure"[[156]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-3-159) was said to have borne Husayn [a son](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Husayn_Zayn_al-Abidin "Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin"), the historical fourth [Shi'a](/wiki/Shi%27a "Shi'a") [imam](/wiki/Imamah_(Shi%27a_doctrine) "Imamah (Shi'a doctrine)"), who insisted that the caliphate rightly belonged to him and his descendants, and that the [Umayyads](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad") had wrongfully wrested it from him. The alleged descent from the Sassanid house counterbalanced the [Arab nationalism](/wiki/Arab_nationalism "Arab nationalism") of the Umayyads, and the Iranian national association with a Zoroastrian past was disarmed. Thus, according to scholar [Mary Boyce](/wiki/Mary_Boyce "Mary Boyce"), "it was no longer the Zoroastrians alone who stood for patriotism and loyalty to the past."[[156]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-3-159) The "damning indictment" that becoming Muslim was [un-Iranian](/wiki/Aniran "Aniran") only remained an idiom in Zoroastrian texts.[[156]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-3-159)

With Iranian support, the [Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasids "Abbasids") overthrew the Umayyads in 750, and in the subsequent caliphate government—that nominally lasted until 1258—Muslim Iranians received marked favor in the new government, both in Iran and at the capital in [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"). This mitigated the antagonism between Arabs and Iranians but sharpened the distinction between Muslims and non-Muslims. The Abbasids zealously persecuted heretics, and although this was directed mainly at Muslim [sectarians](/wiki/Sectarians "Sectarians"), it also created a harsher climate for non-Muslims.[[157]](#cite_note-160)

#### Survival

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/USSHER%281865%29_p012_BAKU%2C_FIRE_TEMPLE_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-USSHER%281865%29_p012_BAKU%2C_FIRE_TEMPLE_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:USSHER(1865)_p012_BAKU,_FIRE_TEMPLE_(cropped).jpg)

The [fire temple](/wiki/Ateshgah_of_Baku "Ateshgah of Baku") of [Baku](/wiki/Baku "Baku"), c. 1860

Despite economic and social incentives to convert, Zoroastrianism remained strong in some regions, particularly in those furthest away from the Caliphate capital at Baghdad. In [Bukhara](/wiki/Bukhara "Bukhara") (present-day [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan")), resistance to Islam required the 9th-century Arab commander [Qutaiba](/wiki/Qutayba_ibn_Muslim "Qutayba ibn Muslim") to convert his province four times. The first three times the citizens reverted to their old religion. Finally, the governor made their religion "difficult for them in every way", turned the local fire temple into a mosque, and encouraged the local population to attend Friday prayers by paying each attendee two [dirhams](/wiki/Dirhams "Dirhams").[[154]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-2-157) The cities where Arab governors resided were particularly vulnerable to such pressures, and in these cases the Zoroastrians were left with no choice but to either conform or migrate to regions that had a more amicable administration.[[154]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-2-157)

The 9th century came to define the great number of Zoroastrian texts that were composed or re-written during the 8th to 10th centuries (excluding copying and lesser amendments, which continued for some time thereafter). All of these works are in the [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian") dialect of that period (free of Arabic words) and written in the difficult [Pahlavi script](/wiki/Pahlavi_script "Pahlavi script") (hence the adoption of the term "Pahlavi" as the name of the variant of the language, and of the genre, of those Zoroastrian books). If read aloud, these books would still have been intelligible to the [laity](/wiki/Laity "Laity"). Many of these texts are responses to the tribulations of the time, and all of them include exhortations to stand fast in their religious beliefs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Atashkadeh.jpg/250px-Atashkadeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atashkadeh.jpg)

[Fire Temple of Yazd](/wiki/Fire_Temple_of_Yazd "Fire Temple of Yazd")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Museum_of_Zoroastrians_-_Kerman.jpg/250px-Museum_of_Zoroastrians_-_Kerman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_of_Zoroastrians_-_Kerman.jpg)

[Museum of Zoroastrians](/wiki/Fire_Temple_of_Kerman "Fire Temple of Kerman") in [Kerman](/wiki/Kerman "Kerman")
