In [Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan") in northeastern Iran, a 10th-century Iranian nobleman brought together four Zoroastrian priests to transcribe a Sassanid-era Middle Persian work titled *Book of the Lord* (*Khwaday Namag*) from Pahlavi script into Arabic script. This transcription, which remained in Middle Persian prose (an Arabic version, by [al-Muqaffa](/wiki/Abdullah_Ibn_al-Muqaffa "Abdullah Ibn al-Muqaffa"), also exists), was completed in 957 and subsequently became the basis for [Firdausi](/wiki/Firdausi "Firdausi")'s *[Book of Kings](/wiki/Shahnameh "Shahnameh")*. It became enormously popular among both Zoroastrians and Muslims, and also served to propagate the Sassanid justification for overthrowing the Arsacids.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Among migrations were those to cities in (or on the margins of) the [great salt deserts](/wiki/Dasht-e_Kavir "Dasht-e Kavir"), in particular to [Yazd](/wiki/Yazd "Yazd") and [Kerman](/wiki/Kerman "Kerman"), which remain centers of Iranian Zoroastrianism to this day. Yazd became the seat of the Iranian high priests during [Mongol Ilkhanate](/wiki/Ilkhanate "Ilkhanate") rule, when the "best hope for survival [for a non-Muslim] was to be inconspicuous."[[158]](#cite_note-161) Crucial to the present-day survival of Zoroastrianism was a migration from the northeastern Iranian town of ["Sanjan in south-western Khorasan"](/wiki/Sanjan_(Khorasan) "Sanjan (Khorasan)"),[[159]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-4-162) to [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat"), in western India. The descendants of that group are today known as the *[Parsis](/wiki/Parsi "Parsi")*—"as the [Gujaratis](/wiki/Gujaratis "Gujaratis"), from long tradition, called anyone from Iran"[[159]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-4-162)—who today represent the larger of the two groups of Zoroastrians in India.[[160]](#cite_note-163)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Special_Container_Carrying_The_Holy_Fire_from_Aden_at_Lonavala.jpg/250px-Special_Container_Carrying_The_Holy_Fire_from_Aden_at_Lonavala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Special_Container_Carrying_The_Holy_Fire_from_Aden_at_Lonavala.jpg)

A special container carrying the holy fire from [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden") to the Lonavala Agiary, India

The struggle between Zoroastrianism and Islam declined in the 10th and 11th centuries. Local Iranian dynasties, "all vigorously Muslim,"[[159]](#cite_note-Boyce_1979-4-162) had emerged as largely independent [vassals](/wiki/Vassals "Vassals") of the Caliphs. In the 16th century, in one of the early letters between Iranian Zoroastrians and their co-religionists in India, the priests of [Yazd](/wiki/Yazd "Yazd") lamented that "no period [in human history], not even [that of Alexander](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period"), had been more grievous or troublesome for the faithful than 'this millennium of the [demon of Wrath](/wiki/Aeshma "Aeshma")'."[[161]](#cite_note-164)

### Modern

Further information: [Parsis](/wiki/Parsis "Parsis"), [Irani (India)](/wiki/Irani_(India) "Irani (India)"), [Zoroastrianism in Iran](/wiki/Zoroastrianism_in_Iran "Zoroastrianism in Iran"), and [Zoroastrianism in India](/wiki/Zoroastrianism_in_India "Zoroastrianism in India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Pundole_Agiary_Udvada.jpg/250px-Pundole_Agiary_Udvada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pundole_Agiary_Udvada.jpg)

A modern Zoroastrian [fire temple](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple") in Western India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Sadeh.jpg/250px-Sadeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sadeh.jpg)

[Sadeh](/wiki/Sadeh "Sadeh") in [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran"), 2011

Zoroastrianism has survived into the modern period, particularly in India, where the Parsis are thought to have been present since about the 9th century.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkjærvø2005-165)

Today Zoroastrianism can be divided in two main schools of thought: reformists and traditionalists. Traditionalists are mostly [Parsis](/wiki/Parsi "Parsi") and accept, beside the Gathas and Avesta, also the [Middle Persian literature](/wiki/Middle_Persian_literature "Middle Persian literature") and like the reformists mostly developed in their modern form from 19th century developments. They generally do not allow [conversion](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion") to the faith and, as such, for someone to be a Zoroastrian they must be born of Zoroastrian parents. Some traditionalists recognize the children of mixed marriages as Zoroastrians, though usually only if the father is a born Zoroastrian.[[163]](#cite_note-CONVERSION_vii-166) Not all Zoroastrians identify with either school. Notable examples gaining traction include Neo-Zoroastrians/Revivalists, which are usually reinterpretations of Zoroastrianism appealing towards Western concerns,[[164]](#cite_note-167) and centering the idea of Zoroastrianism as a living religion. These advocate the revival and maintenance of old rituals and prayers while supporting ethical and social progressive reforms. Both of these latter schools tend to center the Gathas without outright rejecting other texts except the [Vendidad](/wiki/Vendidad "Vendidad").[[165]](#cite_note-168)

From the 19th century onward, the Parsis gained a reputation for their education and widespread influence in all aspects of society. They played an instrumental role in the economic development of the region over many decades; several of the best-known business conglomerates of India are run by Parsi-Zoroastrians, including the [Tata](/wiki/Tata_Group "Tata Group"),[[166]](#cite_note-169) [Godrej](/wiki/Godrej_family "Godrej family"), [Wadia](/wiki/Wadia "Wadia") families, and others.[[167]](#cite_note-170)

For a variety of social and political factors, the Zoroastrians of the Indian subcontinent, namely the Parsis and Iranis, have not engaged in conversion since at least the 18th century. Zoroastrian high priests have historically opined there is no reason to not allow conversion, which is also supported by the [Revayats](/wiki/Revayats "Revayats") and other scripture, though later priests have condemned these judgements.[[168]](#cite_note-171)[[26]](#cite_note-Hinnells-2007-29) Within Iran, many of the beleaguered Zoroastrians have been also historically opposed or not practically concerned with the matter of conversion. Currently though, The Council of Tehran Mobeds (the highest ecclesiastical authority within Iran) endorses conversion but conversion from Islam to Zoroastrianism is illegal under the laws of the Islamic Republic of Iran.[[163]](#cite_note-CONVERSION_vii-166)[[26]](#cite_note-Hinnells-2007-29)

Though the Armenians share a rich history affiliated with Zoroastrianism (that eventually declined with the advent of Christianity), reports indicate that there were Zoroastrians in Armenia until the 1920s.[[169]](#cite_note-172)

At the request of the government of [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") declared 2003 a year to celebrate the "3,000th anniversary of Zoroastrian culture", with special events throughout the world. In 2011, the Tehran Mobeds Anjuman announced that for the first time in the history of modern Iran and of the modern Zoroastrian communities worldwide, women had been ordained in Iran and North America as mobedyars, meaning women assistant [mobeds](/wiki/Mobed "Mobed") (Zoroastrian clergy).[[170]](#cite_note-173)[[171]](#cite_note-174)[[172]](#cite_note-175) The women hold official certificates and can perform the lower-rung religious functions and can initiate people into the religion.[[173]](#cite_note-176)[[174]](#cite_note-Rivetna-177)

The current Zoroastrian population is said be around 100,000 to 200,000 and reportedly declining.[[175]](#cite_note-178) However, further studies are needed to confirm this, as numbers have also been rising in some areas, such as Iran.[[176]](#cite_note-179)[[177]](#cite_note-180)[[178]](#cite_note-181)

## Demographics

Further information: [List of countries by Zoroastrian population](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Zoroastrian_population "List of countries by Zoroastrian population") and [List of Zoroastrians](/wiki/List_of_Zoroastrians "List of Zoroastrians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Zoroastrian_Persian_Shrine_chak-chak_Republic_Ardakan.jpg/250px-Zoroastrian_Persian_Shrine_chak-chak_Republic_Ardakan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zoroastrian_Persian_Shrine_chak-chak_Republic_Ardakan.jpg)

The sacred Zoroastrian pilgrimage shrine of [Chak Chak](/wiki/Chak_Chak,_Yazd "Chak Chak, Yazd") in [Yazd](/wiki/Yazd "Yazd"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")

Zoroastrian communities internationally tend to comprise mostly two main groups of people: [Indian Parsis](/wiki/Parsi_people "Parsi people") and [Iranian Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrians_in_Iran "Zoroastrians in Iran"). According to a study in 2012 by the [Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America](/wiki/Federation_of_Zoroastrian_Associations_of_North_America "Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America"), the number of Zoroastrians worldwide was estimated to be between 111,691 and 121,962. The number is imprecise because of diverging counts in Iran.[[174]](#cite_note-Rivetna-177)

### Iran, Kurdistan Region of Iraq and Central Asia

Main article: [Zoroastrians in Iran](/wiki/Zoroastrians_in_Iran "Zoroastrians in Iran")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Parsi-navjote-sitting.jpg/250px-Parsi-navjote-sitting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parsi-navjote-sitting.jpg)

[Parsi](/wiki/Parsi_people "Parsi people") *[Navjote](/wiki/Navjote "Navjote")* ceremony (rites of admission into the Zoroastrian faith)

Iran's figures of Zoroastrians have ranged widely; the last census (1974) before the [revolution of 1979](/wiki/Revolution_of_1979 "Revolution of 1979") revealed 21,400 Zoroastrians.[[179]](#cite_note-182) Some 10,000 adherents remain in the Central Asian regions that were once considered the traditional stronghold of Zoroastrianism, i.e., [Bactria](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") (see also [Balkh](/wiki/Balkh "Balkh")), which is in Northern Afghanistan; [Sogdiana](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana"); [Margiana](/wiki/Margiana "Margiana"); and other areas close to [Zoroaster's homeland](/wiki/Zoroaster#Place "Zoroaster"). In Iran, emigration, out-marriage and low birth rates are likewise leading to a decline in the Zoroastrian population. Zoroastrian groups in Iran say their number is approximately 60,000.[[180]](#cite_note-183) According to the Iranian census data from 2011 the number of Zoroastrians in Iran was 25,271.[[181]](#cite_note-184)

Communities exist in [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran"), as well as in [Yazd](/wiki/Yazd "Yazd"), [Kerman](/wiki/Kerman "Kerman") and [Kermanshah](/wiki/Kermanshah "Kermanshah"), where many still speak an Iranian language distinct from the usual [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"). They call their language [Dari](/wiki/Dari_(Zoroastrian) "Dari (Zoroastrian)"), not to be confused with the [Dari spoken in Afghanistan](/wiki/Dari_(Persian) "Dari (Persian)"). Their language is also called *Gavri* or *Behdini*, literally "of the Good Religion". Sometimes their language is named for the cities in which it is spoken, such as *Yazdi* or *Kermani*. Iranian Zoroastrians were historically called *[Gabrs](/wiki/Gabr "Gabr")*.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

The number of Kurdish Zoroastrians, along with those of non-ethnic converts, has been estimated differently.[[183]](#cite_note-186) The Zoroastrian Representative of the Kurdistan Regional Government in Iraq has reported that as many as 100,000 people in [Iraqi Kurdistan](/wiki/Iraqi_Kurdistan "Iraqi Kurdistan") have converted to Zoroastrianism recently, with some community leaders speculating that even more Zoroastrians in the region are practicing their faith secretly.[[184]](#cite_note-187)[[185]](#cite_note-188)[[186]](#cite_note-189) However, this has not been confirmed by independent sources.[[187]](#cite_note-190) While, 2024 estimates showed a smaller figure of nearly 5,000 living in the region, primarily in [Sulaymaniya Governorate](/wiki/Sulaymaniya_Governorate "Sulaymaniya Governorate") of Iraq.[[188]](#cite_note-191)

The surge in Kurdish Muslims converting to Zoroastrianism is largely attributed to disillusionment with Islam after experiencing violence and oppression perpetrated by [ISIS](/wiki/ISIS "ISIS") in the area.[[189]](#cite_note-192)

India is considered to be home to a large Zoroastrian population – the descendants of migrants from Iran and today known as the [Parsis](/wiki/Parsi "Parsi"). In India's 2001 census, the Parsi population numbered at 69,601, representing about 0.006% of the total population of India, with a concentration in and around the city of [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"). By 2008, the birth-to-death ratio was 1:5; 200 births per year to 1,000 deaths.[[202]](#cite_note-205) India's 2011 census recorded 57,264 Parsi Zoroastrians.[[200]](#cite_note-The_Hindu-2016-203)

The Zoroastrian population in Pakistan was estimated to number 1,675 people in 2012,[[174]](#cite_note-Rivetna-177) mostly living in [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") (especially [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi")) followed by [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa").[[203]](#cite_note-206)[[204]](#cite_note-207) The [National Database and Registration Authority](/wiki/National_Database_and_Registration_Authority "National Database and Registration Authority") (NADRA) of Pakistan reported that there were 3,650 Parsi voters during the elections in Pakistan in 2013 and 4,235 in 2018.[[205]](#cite_note-208) According to the [2023 Pakistani census](/wiki/2023_Pakistani_census "2023 Pakistani census"), there were 2,348 Parsis across the nation, with 1,656 (70.5%) being located in the [Karachi Division](/wiki/Karachi_Division "Karachi Division").[[206]](#cite_note-209)[[207]](#cite_note-210)

### Western world

See also: [Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America](/wiki/Federation_of_Zoroastrian_Associations_of_North_America "Federation of Zoroastrian Associations of North America") and [Zoroastrianism in the United States](/wiki/Zoroastrianism_in_the_United_States "Zoroastrianism in the United States")

[North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") is thought to be home to 18,000–25,000 Zoroastrians of both South Asian and Iranian backgrounds. As of 2012, the population of Zoroastrians in US was 15,000, making it the third-largest Zoroastrian population in the world after those of India and Iran.[[208]](#cite_note-211) According to the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), the Zoroastrian Canadian population stood at 7,285, of which 3,630 were Parsis (of [South Asian descent](/wiki/South_Asian_Canadians "South Asian Canadians")) and a further 2,390 of [West Asian origin](/wiki/West_Asian_Canadians "West Asian Canadians").[[209]](#cite_note-212) A further 3,500 live in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") (mainly in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")). Stewart, Hinze & Williams write that 3,000 Kurds have converted to Zoroastrianism in Sweden.[[210]](#cite_note-213) According to the [2021 UK census](/wiki/2021_UK_census "2021 UK census"), there were 4,105 Zoroastrians in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), of which 4,043 were in England. The majority (51%) of these (2,050) were in [London](/wiki/London "London"), most notably the boroughs of [Barnet](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Barnet "London Borough of Barnet"), [Harrow](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Harrow "London Borough of Harrow") and [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster"). The remaining 49% of English Zoroastrians were scattered relatively evenly throughout the country, with the second and third largest concentrations being [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") (72) and [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") (47).[[211]](#cite_note-214) In 2020, [Historic England](/wiki/Historic_England "Historic England") published *A Survey of Zoroastrianism Buildings in England* with the aim of providing information about buildings that Zoroastrians use in England so that HE can work with communities to enhance and protect those buildings now and in the future. The scoping survey identified four buildings in England.[[212]](#cite_note-215)

## Relation to other religions and cultures

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Iran-achaemenids_%28darius_the_great%29.jpg/330px-Iran-achaemenids_%28darius_the_great%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iran-achaemenids_(darius_the_great).jpg)

Map of the Achaemenid Empire in the 5th century BCE

### Indo-Iranian origins

See also: [Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") and [Proto-Indo-Iranian religion](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Iranian_religion "Proto-Indo-Iranian religion")

The religion of Zoroastrianism is closest to [historical Vedic religion](/wiki/Historical_Vedic_religion "Historical Vedic religion") to varying degrees.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Some historians believe that Zoroastrianism, along with similar philosophical revolutions in South Asia were interconnected strings of reformation of a common Indo-Aryan thread. Many traits of Zoroastrianism can be traced to prehistoric Indo-Iranian culture and beliefs, that is, before the migrations that separated the [Indo-Aryans](/wiki/Indo-Aryans "Indo-Aryans") and [Iranic](/wiki/Iranics "Iranics") peoples. Thus, Zoroastrianism shares elements with the [historical Vedic religion](/wiki/Historical_Vedic_religion "Historical Vedic religion") that also originated in that era. Some examples include cognates between the [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") word *Ahura* ("Ahura Mazda") and the Vedic [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") word *[Asura](/wiki/Asura "Asura")* ('demon', 'evil demigod'); as well as *[daeva](/wiki/Daeva "Daeva")* ("demon") and *[deva](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)")* ("god") and they both descend from a common [Proto-Indo-Iranian religion](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Iranian_religion "Proto-Indo-Iranian religion").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Zoroastrianism inherited ideas from other belief systems and, like other "practiced" religions, contains [syncretism](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism").[[213]](#cite_note-216) Specifically, Zoroastrianism in [Sogdia](/wiki/Sogdia "Sogdia"), the [Kushan Empire](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"), Armenia, China, and other places incorporates local and foreign practices and deities.[[214]](#cite_note-217)

Conversely, Zoroastrian influenced [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_mythology "Hungarian mythology"), [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_mythology "Slavic mythology"), [Ossetian](/wiki/Ossetian_mythology "Ossetian mythology"), [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_mythology "Turkic mythology") and [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol_mythology "Mongol mythology") mythologies, all of which bear extensive light-dark dualisms and possible sun god theonyms related to [Hvare-khshaeta](/wiki/Hvare-khshaeta "Hvare-khshaeta").[[215]](#cite_note-218)[[216]](#cite_note-219)[[217]](#cite_note-220)

### Abrahamic religions

Zoroastrianism is sometimes credited with being the first monotheistic religion in history,[[21]](#cite_note-auto-24) antedating that of the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") and leaving a lasting and profound imprint on [Second Temple Judaism](/wiki/Second_Temple_Judaism "Second Temple Judaism") and, through it, on later monotheistic religions such as early Christianity and Islam.[[25]](#cite_note-Ferrero-2021-28)[[218]](#cite_note-221)
There are clear commonalities and similarities between Zoroastrianism, Judaism and Christianity, such as: monotheism, dualism (i.e., a robust notion of a Devil—but with a positive appraisal of material creation), symbolism of the divine, heaven(s) and hell(s), angels and demons, eschatology and final judgment, a messianic figure and the idea of a savior, a holy spirit, concern with ritual purity, an idealization of wisdom and righteousness, and other doctrines, symbols, practices, and religious features.[[219]](#cite_note-222)

According to [Mary Boyce](/wiki/Mary_Boyce "Mary Boyce"), "Zoroaster was thus the first to teach the doctrines of an individual judgement, Heaven and Hell, the future resurrection of the body, the general last judgement, and life everlasting for the reunited soul and body. These doctrines were to become familiar articles of faith to much of mankind, through borrowing by Judaism, Christianity and Islam; yet it is in Zoroastrianism itself that they have their fullest logical coherence. Since Zoroaster insisted both on the goodness of material creation, and hence of the physical body, and on the unwavering impartiality of divine justice."[[220]](#cite_note-223)

The interactions between Judaism and Zoroastrianism resulted in transfer of religious ideas between the two religions and as a result, it is believed that Jews under Achaemenid rule were influenced by Zoroastrian angelology, demonology, eschatology, as well as Zoroastrian ideas about compensatory justice in life and after death.[[221]](#cite_note-224) It is also postulated that the Jewish high monotheistic concept of God developed during and after the period of the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"), when the Jews had a prolonged exposure to sophisticated Zoroastrian beliefs.[[222]](#cite_note-225)

In addition, Zoroastrian concepts seeded dualistic ideas in [Jewish eschatology](/wiki/Jewish_eschatology "Jewish eschatology"), such as the belief in a savior, the final battle between good and evil, the triumph of good and the resurrection of the dead. These ideas later passed on to Christianity via Zoroastrian-inspired texts of the Old Testament.[[223]](#cite_note-226)

According to some sources, such as *[The Jewish Encyclopedia](/wiki/The_Jewish_Encyclopedia "The Jewish Encyclopedia")* (1906),[[224]](#cite_note-A._V._Williams_Jackson-1906-227) there exist many similarities between Zoroastrianism and Judaism. This has led some to propose that key Zoroastrian concepts influenced Judaism. However, other scholars disagree, finding that the general social influence of Zoroastrianism was much more limited, and that no link can be found in Jewish or Christian texts.[[225]](#cite_note-Basile-2021-228)

Proponents of a link cite similarities between the two: such as dualism (good and evil, divine twins Ahura Mazda "God" and Angra Mainyu "Satan"), image of the deity, [eschatology](/wiki/Zoroastrian_eschatology "Zoroastrian eschatology"), resurrection and [final judgment](/wiki/Judgement_(afterlife) "Judgement (afterlife)"), [messianism](/wiki/Messianism "Messianism"), [revelation](/wiki/Revelation "Revelation") of Zoroaster on a mountain with [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") on [Mount Sinai](/wiki/Mount_Sinai_(Bible) "Mount Sinai (Bible)"), three sons of [Fereydun](/wiki/Fereydun "Fereydun") with three [sons of Noah](/wiki/Sons_of_Noah "Sons of Noah"), [heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven") and [hell](/wiki/Hell "Hell"), angelology and demonology, cosmology of six days or periods of creation, and [free will](/wiki/Free_will_in_theology "Free will in theology"), among others. Other scholars diminish or reject such influences,[[226]](#cite_note-Nigosian-1993-229)[[227]](#cite_note-Grabbe-2006-230)[[224]](#cite_note-A._V._Williams_Jackson-1906-227)[[228]](#cite_note-231)[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[225]](#cite_note-Basile-2021-228) noting that "Zoroastrianism has a unique theistic doctrine which combines dualism, polytheism and pantheism", rather than being a monotheistic religion.[[225]](#cite_note-Basile-2021-228) Others say "there is little concrete evidence about the precise origin and development of" Zoroastrianism,[[230]](#cite_note-233) and that "Zoroastrianism does not compare with the Jewish belief in the sovereignty of God over the whole of creation."[[231]](#cite_note-234)

Others, such as [Lester L. Grabbe](/wiki/Lester_L._Grabbe "Lester L. Grabbe"), have said "there is general agreement that Persian religion and tradition had its influence on Judaism over the centuries" and the "question is where this influence was and which of the developments in Judaism can be ascribed to the Iranian side as opposed to the effect of the Greek or other cultures".[[227]](#cite_note-Grabbe-2006-230) There exist distinctions but also similarities between Zoroastrian and [Jewish law](/wiki/Jewish_law "Jewish law") regarding marriage and procreation.[[232]](#cite_note-235) While Mary Boyce claims, besides Abrahamic religions, Zoroastrian influence also extended to [Northern Buddhism](/wiki/Northern_Buddhism "Northern Buddhism").[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce20011,_77-236)

#### Islam

Zoroastrians are considered to be a "[People of the Book](/wiki/People_of_the_Book "People of the Book")" by Muslims.[[234]](#cite_note-237)

### Manichaeism

Zoroastrianism is often compared with [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism"). Nominally an Iranian religion, Manichaeism was heavily inspired by Zoroastrianism[[235]](#cite_note-238) because of [Mani](/wiki/Mani_(prophet) "Mani (prophet)")'s Iranian origin, and it was also rooted in prior [Middle-Eastern](/wiki/Middle-East "Middle-East") [Gnostic](/wiki/Gnostic "Gnostic") beliefs.[[226]](#cite_note-Nigosian-1993-229)[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce20011,_77-236)[[227]](#cite_note-Grabbe-2006-230)

Manichaeism adopted many of the [Yazatas](/wiki/Yazata "Yazata") for its own pantheon[[236]](#cite_note-239) Gherardo Gnoli, in *The Encyclopaedia of Religion*,[[237]](#cite_note-240) says that "we can assert that Manichaeism has its roots in the Iranian religious tradition and that its relationship to Mazdaism, or Zoroastrianism, is more or less like that of Christianity to Judaism".[[238]](#cite_note-241)

The two religions have substantial differences.[[239]](#cite_note-242)

### Present-day Iran

Many aspects of Zoroastrianism are present in the culture and mythologies of the peoples of [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran"), not least because Zoroastrianism was a dominant influence on the people of the cultural continent for a thousand years. Even after the rise of Islam and the loss of direct influence, Zoroastrianism remained part of the cultural heritage of the [Iranian language](/wiki/Iranian_language "Iranian language")-speaking world, in part as festivals and customs, but also because [Ferdowsi](/wiki/Ferdowsi "Ferdowsi") incorporated a number of the figures and stories from the [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") in his epic *[Shāhnāme](/wiki/Sh%C4%81hn%C4%81me "Shāhnāme")*, which is pivotal to Iranian identity. One notable example is the incorporation of the Yazata [Sraosha](/wiki/Sraosha "Sraosha") as an angel venerated within [Shia Islam](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") in Iran.[[240]](#cite_note-243)

"In brief, the interpretation we favor is that Zoroastrianism combines cosmogonic dualism and eschatological monotheism in a manner unique to itself among the major religions of the world. This combination results in a religious outlook which cannot be categorized as either straightforward dualism or straightforward monotheism, meaning that the question in the title of this paper poses a false dichotomy. The dichotomy arises, we contend, from a failure to take seriously enough the central role played by time in Zoroastrian theology. Zoroastrianism proclaims a movement through time from dualism toward monotheism, i.e., a dualism which is being made false by the dynamics of time, and a monotheism which is being made true by those same dynamics of time. The meaning of the [eschaton](/wiki/Eschaton "Eschaton") in Zoroastrianism is thus the triumph of monotheism, the good God Ahura Mazdä having at last won his way through to complete and final ascendancy. But in the meantime there is vital truth to dualism, the neglect of which can only lead to a distortion of the religion's essential teachings."
